Obviously there's the base rules, right? You have a stop loss rule. Hey, if you spend X amount, but you haven't got X result, turn it off today continuously, CPA is rise above. You can turn those on. That's basically like what Facebook does is delayed attribution, right? So the problem is that you'll see like a rule turn something off and then it refreshes like 30 minutes later. And then all of a sudden conversions are there that weren't there earlier. And you're ruled and catch it. Cause Facebook really only updates about every 15 minutes. The biggest ones that I've really found that's really helped me with, especially with these accounts that are spending 30, 40 grand a day is the timing rules. So the cool thing about the timing rules is let's say for example, as you can see here even on the slide is saying, if name says reset 12A and ROAS is less than my target ROAS today, then pause it at 12 a.m. Reason for that is, there's a lot of people like if you look in your ad manager, you can actually do a breakdown by time of day on the ad account. And so what I noticed on a lot of my accounts was from 12 a.m. to around 4 a.m. is really not much conversions going on. Hey, if lead cost is above this and it's midnight, go ahead and just pause it. ## [21:11 - 24:11]

over the last seven days, turn on at 4 a.m. And you can customize these rules. And that's how I've really been finding my scales is literally just doubling the budget at 8 a.m., 12 p.m., 3 p.m. and 6 p.m. But every night I'll just put a reset 50 inside the campaigns that have scaled that day. And at midnight, it'll just cut the budget back in half. So that way next day I wake up, I just start back over again and keep scaling up. Bam. That's how I know you're the best dog. The last one that will save people a lot of money in the long run is the lead indicator rules. So you don't want to always just make rules off of, let's say a webinar registration or a lead cost or an application cost because those are so high. Those are so expensive, right? Not as far as like CBO or ad set budgets, but as far as there's standard spend and then there's accelerated spend. Standard spend, Facebook will say, hey, Facebook, let's say you have a $100 budget per day. You go, hey, Facebook, here's $100 and it's a standard spend. Facebook will look for lowest cost conversions, but they also will try to balance your budget out throughout the whole day. So the problem I found, especially on accounts that wanted to spend a lot of money, was Facebook, I knew my best conversions were at 9 a.m., but because Facebook was trying to balance out my budget, it wouldn't spend all during that peak time and so it balance it and I'd lose conversions. Accelerated spend tells Facebook, hey, if there are conversions to be had right now, spend my money. So that's, and that's really why I created the timing rules was because from midnight to 4 a.m., things are so cheap that Facebook would spend all your money in the beginning of the day because it's so cheap during that time because the big companies will turn off their ads, the competition's lower, right? So I basically wanted Facebook, as fast as you can give me the conversions and on my accelerated spends, I set bid caps. ## [33:16 - 36:16]

at one time. So I really like to do that. But yeah, you'll definitely find that smaller budgets typically will get cheaper conversions. The problem with it is, and why I use it more as a stepping stone and not necessarily like a campaign strategy, is it doesn't typically last long.
